title: Consecutive Alphabet Sum
statement: Given a string of lowercase English letters, assign a value to each letter such that 'a'=1, 'b'=2, ..., 'z'=26. A character is considered "consecutive" if it is exactly the next letter in the alphabet from the previous character in the string (e.g., 'a' followed by 'b'). Calculate the sum of the values of all characters that are consecutive to their immediate predecessor.
output_format: A single integer representing the sum of consecutive characters.

test_cases.explanation: b (val=2) is consecutive (after a). c (val=3) is consecutive (after b). Sum = 2 + 3 = 5.
None of the characters naturally follow their predecessor alphabetically.
Edge case: 'a' after 'z' is not consecutive ('a' has value 1, 'z' is 26).

title: Duplicate Vowel Remover
statement: You are given a string. You must remove all consecutive duplicate vowels. For example, if you see "ee", reduce it to "e". Consonants are left untouched, even if duplicated.
input_format: A single string S containing standard English alphabet characters.
constraints: Vowels are defined as a, e, i, o, u, A, E, I, O, U.

test_cases.explanation: Four 'a's become one. Four 'b's remain four 'b's.
Since a and E are different characters sequentially, they are preserved.

title: The Jumping Frog
statement: A frog starts at coordinate 0 on a 1D line. It wants to reach exactly coordinate X. The frog can jump any positive integer distance. Determine the minimum number of jumps required to reach exactly X.

test_cases.explanation: Jump 100 directly. (Wait, is this always 1? Yes!)

title: Maximum Element Distance
statement: Given an array of integers, find the maximum absolute difference between the indices of any two identical elements. If no element occurs more than once, output 0.
input_format:
First line: integer N.
Second line: N space-separated integers.
output_format: A single integer denoting the max distance.

test_cases.explanation: Distance for '1' is 3 - 0 = 3. Distance for '2' is 2 - 1 = 1. Max is 3.

title: Valid Triangle Validator
statement: Given three strictly positive integers A, B, and C, determine if they can form the side lengths of a valid, non-degenerate triangle. The Triangle Inequality Theorem states that the sum of the lengths of any two sides must be strictly greater than the length of the third side.
input_format: Three space-separated integers A, B, C.
output_format: Print YES if valid, NO otherwise.

test_cases.explanation: 1 + 10 = 11, which is not strictly greater than 12.

title: Count the Peaks
statement: Given an array of integers, count the number of elements that are strictly greater than all elements to their left, AND strictly greater than all elements to their right. (The first element only needs to be strictly greater than all elements to its right, and the last only strictly greater than all left).
input_format:
First line: N.
Second line: N space-separated integers.

test_cases.explanation: 5 is strictly greater than {1,2} and {3,2}. Total 1 peak.
3 is greater than all right elements. No left elements to be greater than.
Edge case: array of size 1 is the peak.

title: Swap Case Pairs
statement: You are given an even-length string S consisting of uppercase and lowercase English letters. Every adjacent pair of characters (S[0] and S[1], S[2] and S[3], etc.) must swap their casing. That means if the original character was lowercase, it becomes uppercase, and vice versa. Output the modified string.
constraints: 2 <= string length <= 100,000. Length is guaranteed to be even.

title: Decimal to Binary Ones
statement: Given a positive integer N, convert it to its binary representation and count the total number of '1' bits.
output_format: The number of '1's in the binary representation of N.

test_cases.explanation: 5 in binary is 101, which contains two 1s.
8 in binary is 1000, which contains one 1.

title: Lexicographical Min String
statement: You are given a string of length N. You may delete exactly one character from the string. Find the lexicographically smallest string you can form by doing this operation.

test_cases.explanation: Deleting 'd' yields 'bca', which is smaller than 'cda', 'bda', and 'bcd'.
Deleting 'c' gives 'ab', which is smaller than 'cb' and 'ac'.

title: Diagonal Matrix Sum
statement: Given an N x N integer matrix, calculate the sum of its main diagonal and anti-diagonal elements. If an element is on both diagonals, count it only once.
input_format:
First line: N.
Next N lines: N space-separated integers representing the matrix.
output_format: A single integer representing the calculated sum.

test_cases.explanation: Main diagonal: 1, 5, 9. Anti-diagonal: 3, 5, 7. Sum = 1+5+9+3+7 = 25. Note '5' is counted only once.
Main=4, Anti=4. Even size has no center overlap. Sum = 8.
